timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Siege of Tyre

sections.header: Verses 1-6 — Tyre's Sin: Gloating Over Jerusalem

heb.paragraph: The same mocking exclamation used by Ammon (25:3). Tyre's 'Aha!' over Jerusalem reveals a commercial calculation: with Jerusalem destroyed, Tyre sees an opportunity to capture the inland trade routes. The gloating is not purely sadistic but economically motivated — profit from another's suffering.

refs.note: Solomon's alliance with Hiram of Tyre for building the Temple shows the long history of Israelite-Phoenician economic partnership that makes Tyre's betrayal so bitter.
Amos condemns Tyre for selling Israelites as slaves to Edom, violating a 'covenant of brotherhood' — the trade alliance that should have guaranteed mutual protection.

notes.note: Tyre called Jerusalem 'the gate of the peoples' — recognising Jerusalem's strategic position controlling the land bridge between Egypt, Mesopotamia, and Arabia.
The imagery of 'many nations rising like waves of the sea' is brilliantly suited to the island-city of Tyre: the sea itself becomes the instrument of judgment.
Scraping Tyre 'bare as a rock' (tsahiah sela) puns on the city's name: Tyre (Tsor) means 'rock.' The rock-city will become bare rock.
The 'settlements on the mainland' (banoteha asher basadeh) refers to Tyre's mainland suburb called Ushu (Old Tyre), which was more vulnerable than the island fortress.
Calvin notes that Tyre's sin reveals the spiritual danger of commercial prosperity: wealth breeds a calculating indifference to others' suffering, seeing everything through the lens of profit.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The date formula lacks the month, which is unusual. Some scholars suggest the month was accidentally lost in transmission; others date it to the 11th year (587/586 BC), shortly after Jerusalem's fall.
The phrase 'gate of the peoples' (delet ha'ammim) may refer to Jerusalem's role as a trade gateway between Egypt and Mesopotamia. Tyre saw Jerusalem's destruction as a commercial opportunity.

block.source: Daniel I. Block, Ezekiel, NICOT (2 vols., 1997/1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Block identifies this as the 'introduction oracle' for the three-chapter Tyre series. The opening charge (v.2) establishes the sin; the rest of chapters 26-28 elaborate the consequences. Block notes that the wave metaphor (v.3) is uniquely appropriate for a maritime power.

zimmerli.source: Walther Zimmerli, Ezekiel, Hermeneia (2 vols., 1979/1983) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Zimmerli argues that the Tyre oracles represent Ezekiel's engagement with the question of pagan hubris — the presumption that human achievement (wealth, commerce, technology) can rival or replace divine sovereignty. Tyre is the biblical archetype of civilisational pride.

hist.context: Tyre was the greatest commercial city of the ancient world, a Phoenician island-fortress off the coast of modern Lebanon. It controlled Mediterranean trade for centuries and was fabulously wealthy. Tyre's sin was not idolatry or violence but something more modern: treating Jerusalem's fall as a business opportunity. 'The gate of the peoples is broken; it has swung open to me' (v.2) reveals the merchant's instinct to profit from catastrophe. The Tyre oracles (chs.26-28) are the longest single-nation oracle series in Ezekiel, reflecting the importance and complexity of the Tyre-Israel relationship.

heb.paragraph: Nebuchadnezzar is called 'king of kings' (melekh melakhim) — a title God assigns to a pagan ruler as His instrument of judgment. The title does not imply worship but sovereignty: God raises up and deploys world emperors as tools of His purpose. Daniel will later use the same title for God Himself (Dan 2:47).

refs.note: Jeremiah describes Nebuchadnezzar as God's 'servant' who will judge all nations, including Tyre. Both prophets independently confirm Babylon's role as divine instrument.
Ezekiel's later oracle acknowledges that Nebuchadnezzar received no plunder from Tyre and compensates him with Egypt — a remarkable case of prophetic self-correction.

notes.note: Nebuchadnezzar is called 'king of kings' — a title the Bible assigns to human rulers (Ezra 7:12, Dan 2:37) and ultimately to Christ (Rev 19:16). Here it denotes Babylon's imperial status.
The military details — siege works, battering rams, cavalry — describe standard Babylonian siege warfare attested in Assyrian reliefs and Babylonian chronicles.
The shift from singular ('he') to plural ('they') in v.12 may indicate that the prophecy encompasses not just Nebuchadnezzar but successive conquerors, ultimately including Alexander the Great.
The finality — 'you will never be rebuilt' — was fulfilled: ancient Tyre was never restored to its former glory. The modern city of Tyre (Sur, Lebanon) sits on a different site.

Calvin observes that God calling Nebuchadnezzar 'king of kings' demonstrates that all earthly authority derives from and serves divine sovereignty, even when the ruler is pagan and the purpose is judgment.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET notes that 'king of kings' (melekh melakhim) is an imperial title attested in Mesopotamian inscriptions. Ezekiel uses it factually, not theologically — describing Nebuchadnezzar's political status, not divine endorsement.
The grammatical shift from 3rd person singular to 3rd person plural is debated: some see it as a reference to multiple invasions (Babylonian + later), others as a rhetorical variation.

block.source: Daniel I. Block, Ezekiel, NICOT (2 vols., 1997/1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Block traces the military description's accuracy against Babylonian siege technology documented in archaeological evidence. The 'wall of shields' (v.8), siege ramps (v.8), and battering rams (v.9) all correspond to known Babylonian military engineering.

zimmerli.source: Walther Zimmerli, Ezekiel, Hermeneia (2 vols., 1979/1983) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Zimmerli addresses the 'fulfilment problem': Nebuchadnezzar besieged Tyre for 13 years but never fully conquered the island. Zimmerli argues that the oracle describes the theological reality (Tyre's judgment is decreed) rather than the exact historical mechanism. The final destruction by Alexander (332 BC) completed what Nebuchadnezzar began.

hist.context: Nebuchadnezzar besieged Tyre for 13 years (585-573 BC), the longest siege in ancient history. Ezekiel's oracle describes the siege in vivid military detail: siege ramps, battering rams, cavalry, and the trampling of streets by horses. Historically, Nebuchadnezzar took the mainland settlement but never fully conquered the island fortress — that would await Alexander the Great in 332 BC. This apparent non-fulfilment is addressed in 29:17-20, where God acknowledges that Nebuchadnezzar received no plunder from Tyre and assigns Egypt as compensation.

sections.header: Verses 15-21 — The Descent to the Pit

heb.paragraph: The 'pit' (bor) is the underworld, the realm of the dead. Tyre descends from the heights of commercial glory to the depths of Sheol. The Hebrew conception of death as descent into a pit makes Tyre's fall a cosmic reversal: the city that sat enthroned on the sea sinks below the earth. The same imagery will be developed more fully in chapter 28's Eden/fall narrative.

refs.note: Isaiah's taunt song against Babylon's king uses the same descent-to-Sheol imagery, suggesting a common prophetic tradition for describing the fall of great powers.
Revelation's lament over 'Babylon the Great' draws directly on Ezekiel 26-27's imagery of merchants weeping and coastlands trembling at a great city's fall.

notes.note: The trembling of the coastlands and the stripping of royal robes depict the shock wave that Tyre's fall sends through the ancient commercial world. The entire trading network collapses.
The descent 'to the pit, to the people of long ago' places Tyre among the dead civilisations of antiquity — great cities that once dominated and now exist only in archaeological memory.
The phrase 'you will be sought, but you will never again be found' echoes Psalm 37:36 — the complete erasure of the wicked from history.
Calvin sees in Tyre's descent to the pit a universal truth: no human achievement, however magnificent, can resist divine judgment. Commercial empires are as mortal as the merchants who built them.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The 'people of long ago' (am olam) refers to the ancient dead who inhabit Sheol. The phrase suggests that Tyre will join civilisations already forgotten, losing not just life but memory.

block.source: Daniel I. Block, Ezekiel, NICOT (2 vols., 1997/1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Block classifies this as a 'descent oracle,' a form attested in both Hebrew and Mesopotamian literature. The structure moves from earthly reaction (15-18) to cosmic descent (19-21), placing Tyre's fall in a mythological framework that transcends mere history.

zimmerli.source: Walther Zimmerli, Ezekiel, Hermeneia (2 vols., 1979/1983) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Zimmerli connects the pit imagery to Ezekiel's priestly worldview: the descent from the 'land of the living' to the pit mirrors the movement from sacred (Temple precinct) to profane (outside the camp). Tyre moves from the center of civilisation to its utter periphery.

hist.context: The final section shifts from military description to cosmic lament. The 'coastlands' (maritime trading nations) tremble at Tyre's fall; princes strip off their royal robes and sit in terror. The lament uses descent-to-the-pit language familiar from Mesopotamian mythology (Inanna's descent) and Hebrew Sheol theology. Tyre will join 'those who go down to the pit' — not merely defeated but obliterated from the land of the living. The oracle ends with the chilling declaration: 'I will bring you to a dreadful end and you will be no more.'

themes.note: Sovereignty dominates: God deploys Nebuchadnezzar as His instrument and controls Tyre's cosmic destiny. Judgment and Justice are both high as commercial exploitation meets divine retribution. Prophecy scores high for the precise historical predictions.

rows.label: vv.15-21
rows.text: The Cosmic Consequence: Descent to the Pit

lit.note: Chapter 26 moves from accusation (profit from catastrophe) through military judgment (Nebuchadnezzar's siege) to cosmic consequence (descent to Sheol). The oracle escalates from politics to eschatology.

hebtext.note: The same mocking exclamation used by Ammon (25:3). Tyre's 'Aha!' over Jerusalem reveals a commercial calculation: with Jerusalem destroyed, Tyre sees an opportunity to capture the inland trade routes. The gloating is not purely sadistic but economically motivated — profit from another's suffering.

Nebuchadnezzar is called 'king of kings' (melekh melakhim) — a title God assigns to a pagan ruler as His instrument of judgment. The title does not imply worship but sovereignty: God raises up and deploys world emperors as tools of His purpose. Daniel will later use the same title for God Himself (Dan 2:47).

The 'pit' (bor) is the underworld, the realm of the dead. Tyre descends from the heights of commercial glory to the depths of Sheol. The Hebrew conception of death as descent into a pit makes Tyre's fall a cosmic reversal: the city that sat enthroned on the sea sinks below the earth. The same imagery will be developed more fully in chapter 28's Eden/fall narrative.

trans.title: Comparing translations in Oracle Against Tyre

translations.text: In the eleventh month of the twelfth year, on the first day of the month, the word of the Lord came to me:
In the eleventh year, on the first day of the month, the word of the LORD came to me:
Out in the sea she will become a place to spread fishnets, for I have spoken, declares the Sovereign Lord. She will become plunder for the nations,
She shall be in the midst of the sea a place for the spreading of nets, for I have spoken, declares the Lord GOD. And she shall become plunder for the nations,

debate: The date formula lacks the month, which is unusual. Some scholars suggest the month was accidentally lost in transmission; others date it to the 11th …

Reads Oracle Against Tyre within the canonical framework of fulfilled prophecy and covenant theology.
Represented by Calvin, MacArthur, and the evangelical tradition.
Both readings engage the text seriously; they differ primarily in their hermeneutical starting points and assumptions about authorship and historical context.
The grammatical shift from 3rd person singular to 3rd person plural is debated: some see it as a reference to multiple invasions (Babylonian + later),…

thread.text: Solomon's alliance with Hiram of Tyre for building the Temple shows the long history of Israelite-Phoenician economic partnership that makes Tyre's betrayal so bitter.
Amos condemns Tyre for selling Israelites as slaves to Edom, violating a 'covenant of brotherhood' — the trade alliance that should have guaranteed mutual protection.
Jeremiah describes Nebuchadnezzar as God's 'servant' who will judge all nations, including Tyre. Both prophets independently confirm Babylon's role as divine instrument.
Ezekiel's later oracle acknowledges that Nebuchadnezzar received no plunder from Tyre and compensates him with Egypt — a remarkable case of prophetic self-correction.
Isaiah's taunt song against Babylon's king uses the same descent-to-Sheol imagery, suggesting a common prophetic tradition for describing the fall of great powers.
Revelation's lament over 'Babylon the Great' draws directly on Ezekiel 26-27's imagery of merchants weeping and coastlands trembling at a great city's fall.

coaching.tip: Tyre's sin is specific: 'Aha! The gate to the nations is broken, and its doors have swung open to me' (v. 2). Tyre saw Jerusalem's fall as a commercial opportunity — one competitor eliminated. Ezekiel condemns the reduction of human catastrophe to market advantage. Profiting from another's disaster draws divine attention. The oracle warns against economic logic that has no moral dimension.

Tyre's descent 'to the pit' (v. 20) places the commercial superpower among the dead in the underworld — the same fate reserved for empires throughout Ezekiel 28–32. The 'pit' language creates a counter-geography: beneath the visible world of trade routes and harbors lies a realm where all human power is equalized. Ezekiel's Sheol is democracy by death — no empire retains its status underground.
